verses.text: In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth.
verses.commentary: Genesis opens with a sovereign declaration: creation is intentional, ordered, and attributed to God alone.

original.text: bereshit bara elohim et hashamayim ve-et haaretz

keyLayers.literal: God is the first cause and creator of all that exists.
keyLayers.historical: Written within Israel's theological tradition to distinguish Yahweh from surrounding creation myths.
keyLayers.prophetic: Sets the frame for new creation motifs fulfilled in Messiah-centered restoration.
keyLayers.symbolic: Beginning symbolizes divine order emerging from unformed reality.

verses.text: The blood shall be a sign for you, on the houses where you are.
verses.commentary: Passover blood marks covenant protection and points forward to redemptive sacrifice.

original.text: vehayah hadam lakem leot al habattim

keyLayers.literal: Blood on doorposts identifies households under divine protection.
keyLayers.historical: Given during Israel's liberation from Egypt as a ritual act of obedience.
keyLayers.prophetic: Foreshadows the Messiah as the sacrificial Lamb.
keyLayers.symbolic: Blood signifies life, atonement, and covenant identity.

verses.text: The Lord is my shepherd; I shall not want.
verses.commentary: David uses shepherd imagery to present God as personal guide, protector, and provider.

original.text: adonai roi lo ehsar

keyLayers.literal: God personally provides and guides the believer.
keyLayers.historical: Pastoral imagery reflects ancient Near Eastern leadership metaphors.
keyLayers.prophetic: Points toward the Good Shepherd identity in the New Testament.
keyLayers.symbolic: Shepherd expresses intimate governance and covenant care.

verses.text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.
verses.commentary: John identifies the pre-existent Word as divine and active in creation.

original.text: en arche en ho logos kai ho logos en pros ton theon kai theos en ho logos

keyLayers.literal: The Word existed eternally and is fully divine.
keyLayers.historical: Written in a Hellenistic context where logos carried philosophical and theological significance.
keyLayers.prophetic: Links Genesis creation language to Messiah revelation.
keyLayers.symbolic: Word symbolizes God's self-disclosure in person and power.

verses.text: Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world!
verses.commentary: John the Baptist introduces Jesus as sacrificial fulfillment.

original.text: ide ho amnos tou theou ho airon ten hamartian tou kosmou

keyLayers.literal: Jesus is identified as the sacrificial one who removes sin.
keyLayers.historical: Announced in a Jewish purification context with Passover resonances.
keyLayers.prophetic: Echoes Isaiah's suffering servant and Passover fulfillment.
keyLayers.symbolic: Lamb represents innocence, sacrifice, and covenant redemption.

verses.text: There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.
verses.commentary: Paul announces legal and relational freedom for believers united with Christ.

original.text: ouden ara nyn katakrima tois en christo iesou

keyLayers.literal: Believers in Christ are no longer under condemnation.
keyLayers.historical: Addressed to a mixed Jewish-Gentile church navigating law and grace.
keyLayers.prophetic: Shows covenant fulfillment through Messiah's redemptive work.
keyLayers.symbolic: No condemnation symbolizes release from spiritual verdict and shame.

verses.text: For Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed.
verses.commentary: Paul explicitly identifies Christ as Passover fulfillment.

original.text: kai gar to pascha hemon etuthe christos

keyLayers.literal: Christ is presented as the sacrificial Passover lamb.
keyLayers.historical: Written to correct church practice using familiar festival typology.
keyLayers.prophetic: Connects Old Testament sacrificial system to Messiah's final offering.
keyLayers.symbolic: Passover symbolizes deliverance and moral cleansing.

verses.text: Then I saw a new heaven and a new earth.
verses.commentary: The biblical storyline culminates in renewed creation and restored dwelling.

original.text: kai eidon ouranon kainon kai gen kainen

keyLayers.literal: A renewed cosmos replaces the former order.
keyLayers.historical: Addressed to persecuted churches with hope-centered apocalyptic imagery.
keyLayers.prophetic: Completes promises of restoration in prophets and gospel trajectory.
keyLayers.symbolic: New heavens and earth symbolize total renewal under God's reign.

verses.text: Fear not, for I am with you; be not dismayed, for I am your God.
verses.commentary: A covenant assurance of divine presence and strengthening in fear-producing circumstances.

original.text: al-tira ki imkha ani

keyLayers.literal: God commands His people not to fear because He is with them.
keyLayers.historical: Addressed to a vulnerable covenant people under geopolitical threats.
keyLayers.prophetic: Extends to Messianic-era confidence in God's sustaining presence.
keyLayers.symbolic: Fear yields to covenant trust and divine nearness.

verses.text: For God gave us a spirit not of fear but of power and love and self-control.
verses.commentary: Paul frames Christian courage as Spirit-formed power, love, and disciplined faithfulness.

original.text: ou gar edoken hemin ho theos pneuma deilias

keyLayers.literal: The Spirit empowers believers away from fear and into faithful strength.
keyLayers.historical: Written amid ministry pressure and opposition to sustain courage.
keyLayers.prophetic: Reflects the Spirit-enabled endurance promised to God's people.
keyLayers.symbolic: Fear is displaced by covenantal courage and disciplined love.

verses.text: Trust in the Lord with all your heart, and do not lean on your own understanding.
verses.commentary: Wisdom literature directs total reliance on God above autonomous judgment.

original.text: betach el-yhwh bekhol-libbekha

keyLayers.literal: Wholehearted trust in God should govern decisions.
keyLayers.historical: Part of covenant wisdom instruction shaping faithful life practice.
keyLayers.prophetic: Anticipates renewed-heart obedience fulfilled in covenant transformation.
keyLayers.symbolic: Heart-level trust reorders reasoning under divine wisdom.

verses.text: For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God.
verses.commentary: Salvation is presented as divine gift received by faith, not human achievement.

original.text: te gar chariti este sesosmenoi dia pisteos

keyLayers.literal: Grace is the source of salvation and faith is the means of reception.
keyLayers.historical: Addresses identity and boasting issues in a mixed church setting.
keyLayers.prophetic: Reveals covenant mercy fulfilled in Messiah's redemptive work.
keyLayers.symbolic: Gift language highlights dependence on divine initiative.

verses.text: Not a result of works, so that no one may boast.
verses.commentary: Paul excludes boasting by denying works as the basis of salvation.

original.text: ouk ex ergon hina me tis kauchesetai

keyLayers.literal: Works cannot be the ground of salvation.
keyLayers.historical: Counters status-based boasting in first-century community dynamics.
keyLayers.prophetic: Emphasizes salvation as God's covenant act in Messiah.
keyLayers.symbolic: Boasting is replaced by grateful trust.

verses.text: So also faith by itself, if it does not have works, is dead.
verses.commentary: James teaches that genuine faith necessarily expresses itself through obedient action.

original.text: he pistis ean me eche erga nekra estin

keyLayers.literal: Faith that lacks visible fruit is functionally lifeless.
keyLayers.historical: Addresses communities where confession and conduct diverged.
keyLayers.prophetic: Echoes covenant calls to embodied righteousness.
keyLayers.symbolic: Living faith demonstrates itself through obedience.

verses.text: I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and her offspring; he shall bruise your head, and you shall bruise his heel.
verses.commentary: The Protoevangelium — the first messianic promise in Scripture, anticipating the cosmic conflict and ultimate victory of the Seed.

original.text: vehu yeshufkha rosh veattah teshufenu akev

keyLayers.literal: God declares enmity between the serpent and the woman's descendant, with the Seed ultimately victorious.
keyLayers.historical: Delivered in the context of the primal covenant rupture — the first word of grace after sin.
keyLayers.prophetic: Points forward to Messiah's victory over sin, death, and the enemy through his own suffering.
keyLayers.symbolic: The bruised heel and crushed head reveal substitutionary victory through suffering.

verses.text: For the life of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it for you on the altar to make atonement for your souls, for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life.
verses.commentary: Establishes the theological principle that blood = life, and that atonement requires its offering.

original.text: ki nefesh habasar badam hi vaani netattiv lakhem

keyLayers.literal: Blood represents life; giving blood on the altar effects atonement.
keyLayers.historical: Central principle of the entire Levitical sacrificial system.
keyLayers.prophetic: Explains why Messiah's blood specifically accomplishes what no animal sacrifice could ultimately complete.
keyLayers.symbolic: Blood as life poured out is the ultimate covenant currency for reconciliation.

verses.text: But he was pierced for our transgressions; he was crushed for our iniquities; upon him was the chastisement that brought us peace, and with his wounds we are healed.
verses.commentary: The prophetic pinnacle of the Suffering Servant passage — the most quoted OT text in the New Testament.

original.text: vehu mecholal mipesha'enu medukka me'avonoteinu

keyLayers.literal: The servant suffers vicariously — our transgressions become his wounds.
keyLayers.historical: Written 700+ years before Messiah, in a context of national sin and impending judgment.
keyLayers.prophetic: Directly fulfilled in the passion and crucifixion of Jesus.
keyLayers.symbolic: Piercing and healing form the paradox of substitutionary atonement.

verses.text: But you, O Bethlehem Ephrathah, who are too little to be among the clans of Judah, from you shall come forth for me one who is to be ruler in Israel, whose coming forth is from of old, from ancient days.
verses.commentary: A precise geographical and eternal-origins prophecy of Messiah's birth, delivered 700 years before the event.

original.text: uveyt lechem ephratah tzair lihyot bealfei yehuda

keyLayers.literal: Bethlehem — a small, insignificant town — is named as Messiah's birthplace.
keyLayers.historical: Spoken to a small province under threat; the divine reversal of the despised becoming great.
keyLayers.prophetic: Fulfilled at the nativity; the 'ancient days' clause affirms Messiah's pre-existence.
keyLayers.symbolic: Smallness as divine vehicle — God consistently works through the humble and overlooked.

verses.text: And he believed the LORD, and he counted it to him as righteousness.
verses.commentary: The foundational justification-by-faith text — the pattern Paul builds his entire Romans 4 argument from.

original.text: veheamin bayhwh vayachshevehah lo tzedakah

keyLayers.literal: Abraham trusted God's promise and this trust was credited as righteousness — not his works.
keyLayers.historical: Occurs before circumcision (Genesis 17) — Paul's key argument that faith, not ritual, justifies.
keyLayers.prophetic: The pattern of faith-righteousness defines the covenant basis for all of Scripture.
keyLayers.symbolic: Belief as the act of spiritual embrace — receiving what one cannot earn.

verses.text: Now faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen.
verses.commentary: The Bible's most precise definition of faith — not mere opinion, but certainty about unseen realities.

original.text: estin de pistis elpizomenon hypostasis pragmaton elegchos ou blepomenon

keyLayers.literal: Faith is the substance of hope and the proof of unseen realities — not wishful thinking.
keyLayers.historical: Written to Jewish Christians tempted to abandon faith under pressure.
keyLayers.prophetic: Points forward to the full revelation of what faith now perceives dimly.
keyLayers.symbolic: Hypostasis (substance/title deed) — faith is the legal reality of future promise.

verses.text: For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son, that whoever believes in him should not perish but have eternal life.
verses.commentary: The gospel in a single verse — divine motivation (love), divine act (gave), human response (believe), and outcome (eternal life).

original.text: houtos gar egapesen ho theos ton kosmon hosti ton huion autou ton monogene edoken

keyLayers.literal: God's love motivated the sacrifice of the Son — belief leads to life, not perishing.
keyLayers.historical: Spoken to a Jewish religious leader seeking to understand the new birth.
keyLayers.prophetic: Fulfills Genesis 3:15 and Isaiah 53:5 — the promised gift of life through substitution.
keyLayers.symbolic: The world (kosmos) — not Israel alone — is the scope of divine redemptive love.

verses.text: For all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.
verses.commentary: Paul's universal diagnosis — no one, Jew or Gentile, stands clear of the verdict of sin.

original.text: pantes gar hemarton kai hysterountai tes doxes tou theou

keyLayers.literal: Every person has sinned and thereby forfeits the glory they were made to reflect.
keyLayers.historical: Part of Paul's argued case establishing universal need for the gospel.
keyLayers.prophetic: Anticipates the universal scope of redemption declared in Romans 8:1.
keyLayers.symbolic: Missing glory — mankind was created to mirror divine reality; sin distorts that image.

verses.text: For the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.
verses.commentary: The sharpest contrast in Paul: what sin earns vs. what grace freely gives — death vs. life.

original.text: ta gar opsonia tes hamartias thanatos to de charisma tou theou zoe aionios

keyLayers.literal: Sin pays wages (what is owed); God gives a gift (what is unearned). The contrast is total.
keyLayers.historical: Military imagery: opsonia was soldier's pay — sin is a deadly employer.
keyLayers.prophetic: The free gift of life anticipates Revelation's new creation where death is no more.
keyLayers.symbolic: Wages versus gift — earning versus receiving — captures the whole grace-works debate.

verses.text: For I delivered to you as of first importance what I also received: that Christ died for our sins in accordance with the Scriptures.
verses.commentary: Paul's creedal summary — the earliest recorded statement of the gospel, explicitly anchored to Old Testament Scripture.

original.text: paredoka gar hymin en protois ho kai parelabon hoti christos apethanen

keyLayers.literal: The core gospel: Messiah's death for sin is anchored to specific Old Testament promises.
keyLayers.historical: Likely a pre-Pauline creed, dating very early — possibly within years of the resurrection.
keyLayers.prophetic: Confirms that the cross was not an accident but the fulfillment of the scriptural plan.
keyLayers.symbolic: Died 'for our sins' — substitutionary exchange is the grammar of salvation.
